sections.heading: What is Gson?
sections.content: Gson is a Java/Kotlin library developed by Google for converting objects to JSON strings (serialization) and JSON strings back into objects (deserialization). In Android networking, Gson is most commonly used as the converter factory for Retrofit — it automatically maps JSON API responses into your Kotlin data classes and vice versa. Gson works by inspecting field names and types using reflection and matching them to JSON keys. While newer libraries like Moshi and kotlinx.serialization offer better Kotlin support, Gson remains the most widely used JSON library in Android due to its simplicity and zero-configuration default behavior.

sections.heading: Why Use Gson?
items: Zero configuration — works out of the box with no annotations required for simple data classes
Widely adopted — used in millions of Android apps; extensive documentation and community support
Retrofit integration — GsonConverterFactory plugs directly into Retrofit with a single line
Flexible — supports nested objects, generics, custom serializers, and type adapters
Handles null values — can serialize and deserialize null fields without crashing
Supports @SerializedName — maps JSON keys with different names (snake_case) to Kotlin properties (camelCase)
Bidirectional — the same configuration handles both serialization (object → JSON) and deserialization (JSON → object)
Handles unknown fields gracefully — ignores JSON keys that have no matching property in the data class

rows.gson: Limited — uses Java reflection; no Kotlin null safety awareness
rows.kotlinx_serialization: Native Kotlin — designed specifically for Kotlin

rows.gson: Poor — can set non-null Kotlin fields to null from JSON
Ignores Kotlin default values — sets field to null/0 if missing in JSON
rows.moshi: Respects Kotlin default values with KotlinJsonAdapterFactory
rows.kotlinx_serialization: Fully supports Kotlin default parameter values

rows.gson: Reflection at runtime — no code generation needed
rows.moshi: Optional — KSP/kapt for codegen or reflection at runtime
rows.kotlinx_serialization: Compile-time code generation via Kotlin compiler plugin

rows.moshi: Faster with codegen; slower with reflection
rows.kotlinx_serialization: Fastest — compile-time generated adapters, no reflection

rows.gson: Lowest — zero config for basic use
rows.moshi: Medium — requires KotlinJsonAdapterFactory or KSP setup
rows.kotlinx_serialization: Medium — requires compiler plugin and @Serializable annotation

rows.moshi: Kotlin projects needing better safety than Gson

sections.heading: Basic Gson Usage
sections.content: Gson can be used standalone — independent of Retrofit — for manual JSON parsing and serialization. The core Gson object is created using Gson() or GsonBuilder().create(). Deserialization converts a JSON string into a Kotlin object using fromJson(). Serialization converts a Kotlin object into a JSON string using toJson(). These operations are synchronous and should always be performed on a background thread when dealing with large payloads.

sections.heading: Core Gson Operations
items: Create a Gson instance: val gson = Gson() — default configuration
Create with custom config: val gson = GsonBuilder().setDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd").create()
Deserialize JSON to object: val user = gson.fromJson(jsonString, User::class.java)
Deserialize JSON to list: val users = gson.fromJson(jsonString, Array<User>::class.java).toList()
Deserialize with TypeToken for generic types: val type = object : TypeToken<List<User>>() {}.type; val users = gson.fromJson(jsonString, type)
Serialize object to JSON: val json = gson.toJson(user)
Serialize list to JSON: val json = gson.toJson(userList)
Pretty-print JSON: val gson = GsonBuilder().setPrettyPrinting().create(); gson.toJson(user)
Parse JSON to JsonObject (dynamic): val obj = gson.fromJson(jsonString, JsonObject::class.java)

sections.heading: JSON to Kotlin Data Class — Basic Mapping

rows.notes: JSON integer maps to Kotlin Int or Long
JSON string maps to Kotlin String
JSON boolean maps to Kotlin Boolean; field name mismatch requires @SerializedName
JSON decimal maps to Double or Float
JSON null maps to Kotlin nullable type; use ? to avoid NullPointerException
JSON array of strings maps to List<String>
Nested JSON object maps to a nested Kotlin data class
JSON array of objects maps to List<DataClass>

sections.heading: @SerializedName — Mapping JSON Keys to Kotlin Properties
sections.content: REST APIs commonly use snake_case for JSON keys (e.g., first_name, created_at, is_active) while Kotlin conventions use camelCase for property names (firstName, createdAt, isActive). Gson's @SerializedName annotation bridges this gap by specifying the exact JSON key name for a Kotlin property. When the JSON key and the Kotlin property name match exactly, no annotation is needed. When they differ, @SerializedName is required for correct mapping.

sections.heading: @SerializedName Usage Patterns
items: Basic mapping: @SerializedName("first_name") val firstName: String
Boolean with prefix mismatch: @SerializedName("is_active") val isActive: Boolean
Timestamp field: @SerializedName("created_at") val createdAt: String
Alternate names (Gson 2.4+): @SerializedName(value = "userId", alternate = ["user_id", "uid"]) val userId: Int
The alternate array lets Gson match multiple possible JSON key names to the same field
Nested @SerializedName: apply the annotation at every level where the key differs
Without @SerializedName: Gson uses the exact Kotlin property name as the JSON key
FieldNamingPolicy is an alternative to @SerializedName applied globally: GsonBuilder().setFieldNamingPolicy(FieldNamingPolicy.LOWER_CASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES)

rows.behavior: Uses the field name exactly as declared — no transformation
Converts camelCase to snake_case automatically for all fields
Capitalizes the first letter of each field name
Capitalizes and adds spaces between words

sections.heading: GsonBuilder — Customizing Gson Behavior
sections.content: The default Gson() instance handles most use cases, but GsonBuilder provides fine-grained control over serialization and deserialization behavior. Use GsonBuilder when you need to configure date formats, null serialization, field naming policies, pretty printing, lenient parsing, or custom type adapters. The configured Gson instance should be a singleton — create it once and reuse it across your app.

sections.heading: GsonBuilder Configuration Options
items: setDateFormat("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss") — parses ISO 8601 date strings into java.util.Date objects automatically
serializeNulls() — by default Gson skips null fields in serialization; this option includes them
setPrettyPrinting() — formats output JSON with indentation for readability (use in debug only)
setFieldNamingPolicy(FieldNamingPolicy.LOWER_CASE_WITH_UNDERSCORES) — global snake_case conversion
excludeFieldsWithoutExposeAnnotation() — only serialize/deserialize fields annotated with @Expose
registerTypeAdapter(Date::class.java, dateAdapter) — registers a custom TypeAdapter for specific types
registerTypeHierarchyAdapter(Animal::class.java, adapter) — handles polymorphic types
setLenient() — parses malformed JSON that violates strict JSON spec (use with caution)
disableHtmlEscaping() — prevents escaping of special HTML characters like < > & in JSON strings

rows.code: val type = object : TypeToken<List<User>>() {}.type; val users = gson.fromJson(json, type)
rows.notes: TypeToken captures generic type information at runtime — required for generic collections

rows.code: val type = object : TypeToken<Map<String, User>>() {}.type; val map = gson.fromJson(json, type)
rows.notes: Required for any generic type — Gson cannot infer the type from Class alone

rows.code: val type = object : TypeToken<ApiResponse<List<User>>>() {}.type; val response = gson.fromJson(json, type)
rows.notes: Nested generics like ApiResponse<List<User>> also require TypeToken

rows.code: suspend fun getUsers(): List<User> — Retrofit + Gson handle TypeToken internally
rows.notes: Retrofit automatically resolves generic return types — no manual TypeToken needed

sections.heading: Custom TypeAdapters
items: A TypeAdapter tells Gson exactly how to read and write a specific type — overrides default behavior
Extend TypeAdapter<YourType> and implement write(writer, value) and read(reader): YourType
Register with: GsonBuilder().registerTypeAdapter(YourType::class.java, YourTypeAdapter())
Use case: parsing a Date from a Unix timestamp integer instead of a formatted string
Use case: parsing a Boolean from a 0/1 integer API that returns numbers instead of true/false
Use case: polymorphic JSON where the type field determines which subclass to deserialize into
JsonDeserializer<T> is a simpler alternative to TypeAdapter for deserialization only
JsonSerializer<T> is the complementary interface for serialization only

rows.challenge: API uses snake_case, Kotlin uses camelCase
Date field comes as a formatted string
Date field comes as a Unix timestamp (Long)
rows.solution: Keep the field as Long in the data class and convert manually, or use a custom TypeAdapter

rows.challenge: Non-null Kotlin field receives null from JSON
rows.solution: Make the field nullable (String?) or provide a default value; consider switching to Moshi or kotlinx.serialization

rows.challenge: Kotlin data class default values ignored
rows.solution: Gson ignores Kotlin defaults — use @SerializedName with a fallback or switch to Moshi/kotlinx.serialization

rows.challenge: API response wraps data in a generic envelope
rows.solution: Create a generic wrapper: data class ApiResponse<T>(val data: T, val status: String)

rows.challenge: JSON field name is a Kotlin reserved word
rows.solution: @SerializedName("class") val className: String — rename in Kotlin, map to original in JSON

rows.challenge: Polymorphic types — different subclasses based on a type field
rows.solution: Register a RuntimeTypeAdapterFactory or custom JsonDeserializer to handle type discrimination

sections.heading: Gson with Retrofit — Integration
items: Add the converter: Retrofit.Builder().addConverterFactory(GsonConverterFactory.create())
Pass a custom Gson instance: GsonConverterFactory.create(customGson) — use this for date formats and field naming
Retrofit uses Gson to automatically deserialize the response body into your return type
Retrofit uses Gson to automatically serialize @Body parameters into JSON
No manual fromJson() or toJson() calls needed — Retrofit and Gson handle everything
For List return types: suspend fun getUsers(): List<User> — Gson handles the array via Retrofit's TypeToken resolution
Error body is NOT automatically parsed — use gson.fromJson(e.response()?.errorBody()?.string(), ApiError::class.java) manually
Null responses from the API (204 No Content) should use Response<Unit> not Unit — to avoid NullPointerException

rows.description: Call excludeFieldsWithoutExposeAnnotation() on GsonBuilder — only @Expose fields are included
Marks a field for both serialization and deserialization — equivalent to @Expose(serialize = true, deserialize = true)
Field is deserialized from JSON but not included when serializing to JSON
Field is included when serializing to JSON but ignored when parsing from JSON
Field is excluded from both serialization and deserialization when filtering is enabled

sections.heading: Parsing Dynamic or Unknown JSON
items: Use JsonObject when the JSON structure is unknown or dynamic at compile time
Parse to JsonObject: val obj = gson.fromJson(jsonString, JsonObject::class.java)
Read a field: obj.get("name")?.asString
Check if field exists: obj.has("optionalField")
Get nested object: obj.getAsJsonObject("address").get("city")?.asString
Get array: obj.getAsJsonArray("tags").map { it.asString }
Use JsonElement as a property type in data classes for fields that can be any JSON value
For semi-dynamic responses, use Map<String, JsonElement> as a data class field type

rows.represents: A JSON string, number, or boolean

rows.access_method: isJsonNull() to check; no value to extract

sections.heading: Gson Pitfalls Specific to Kotlin
items: Non-null Kotlin fields can be set to null by Gson — Gson does not respect Kotlin's type system
Kotlin data class default values are ignored — Gson bypasses the constructor entirely using reflection
val fields can be set by Gson despite being immutable in Kotlin — Gson uses reflection to write to fields
data class with no-arg constructor required for some use cases — use default values for all parameters
Sealed classes and interfaces are not supported by Gson natively — use custom TypeAdapter
object (singleton) classes are not deserialized correctly — Gson creates a new instance instead
Kotlin companion object fields are excluded by Gson — this is usually desired behavior
Use @Transient to exclude a field from Gson: @Transient val computedValue: String = "" — Gson ignores @Transient fields

rows.mistake: Not using @SerializedName for snake_case API fields
rows.fix: Add @SerializedName("field_name") or configure FieldNamingPolicy globally in GsonBuilder

rows.mistake: Using non-nullable Kotlin types for fields that can be null in JSON
rows.fix: Always use nullable types (String?, Int?) for JSON fields that may be absent or null

rows.mistake: Relying on Kotlin default parameter values with Gson
rows.fix: Gson ignores defaults — either use @SerializedName fallback or switch to Moshi/kotlinx.serialization
Use object : TypeToken<List<User>>() {}.type for generic deserialization outside Retrofit

rows.mistake: Parsing JSON on the main thread
rows.fix: Run fromJson() inside withContext(Dispatchers.Default) for large payloads

rows.mistake: Creating a new Gson instance for every parse operation
rows.fix: Gson instances are thread-safe — create once as a singleton and reuse

rows.mistake: Trying to parse error bodies automatically with Retrofit + Gson
rows.fix: Error bodies must be parsed manually: gson.fromJson(e.response()?.errorBody()?.string(), ApiError::class.java)

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Gson is the most widely used JSON library in Android, offering zero-configuration JSON parsing that integrates seamlessly with Retrofit through GsonConverterFactory. Use @SerializedName to handle snake_case API responses, GsonBuilder for custom date formats and field naming policies, and TypeToken for generic type deserialization outside Retrofit. Be aware of Gson's Kotlin limitations — particularly around null safety and default values — and consider Moshi or kotlinx.serialization for new Kotlin-first projects where compile-time type safety is a priority. Regardless of which library you choose, understanding JSON parsing fundamentals makes you effective with any of them.
